cases.name: int and double render differently when inlined
cases.note: The single most important case in the corpus, and the reason input values carry a type tag. `integral_double` is the trap: a double whose value happens to be integral (5.0). JavaScript cannot represent the difference from int 5 at all, so it emits `@p0 tinyint` and `= 5` for BOTH — and that is the frozen contract. Dart CAN represent the difference, and by default would emit `@p0 float` and `= 5.0` for the double. Worse, Dart disagrees with itself: `5.0 is int` is false on the Dart VM but true on dart2js, and `(5.0).toString()` is '5.0' on the VM but '5' on the web. So a naive Dart port fails this case on Flutter mobile and passes it on Flutter web. Dart must normalize an integral double to its integer rendering. Only `d` (5.5, genuinely fractional) may render as a float.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[i] = @p0 AND [m].[integral_double] = @p1 AND [m].[d] = @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 float', @p0 = 5, @p1 = 5, @p2 = 5.5;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[i] = 5 AND [m].[integral_double] = 5 AND [m].[d] = 5.5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`i` = ? AND `m`.`integral_double` = ? AND `m`.`d` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`i` = 5 AND `m`.`integral_double` = 5 AND `m`.`d` = 5.5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = $1 AND "m"."integral_double" = $2 AND "m"."d" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = 5 AND "m"."integral_double" = 5 AND "m"."d" = 5.5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = ? AND "m"."integral_double" = ? AND "m"."d" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = 5 AND "m"."integral_double" = 5 AND "m"."d" = 5.5;

cases.name: select all with a where on a bound boolean
cases.note: The seed case 'select all from table' plus one WHERE, and the pair now pins that a WHERE changes nothing except the WHERE. Until 7.0.0 it changed a great deal: MSSQL injected a safety-net `TOP (1000)` on an unbounded outer SELECT, and any WHERE state suppressed it — so this op-list and the seed case had genuinely different MSSQL shapes. 7.0.0 removed the cap, so all four dialects now differ from the seed case only by the appended predicate. What is left under test is the bound boolean: MSSQL declares `@p0 bit` and passes `@p0 = 1`, not `true`, while the other three bind their own placeholder shape (`?`, `$1`, `?`) and carry the value out in `params`. Raw mode inlines the literal `true` on all four.

cases.name: select distinct with select all
cases.note: `SELECT DISTINCT *`, identical on all four dialects. Before 7.0.0 this case carried the DISTINCT/TOP token-order contract: MSSQL's automatic cap made the golden `SELECT DISTINCT TOP (1000) *`, which proved the TOP hook runs after the DISTINCT keyword and before the column list. The cap is gone, so that ordering is now observable only through an explicit `.top(n)` — see the next case, where MSSQL emits `SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) *`. Reversing the two (`SELECT TOP (10) DISTINCT *`) is still a T-SQL syntax error; this case simply no longer demonstrates it.

cases.name: select distinct with an explicit top
cases.note: `.top(n)` renders through the `beforeSelectColumns` hook, which defaultSelect calls after it has written `SELECT ` and the `DISTINCT ` keyword and before the column list — hence `SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) *`. Emitting `SELECT TOP (10) DISTINCT *` instead is a T-SQL syntax error. MSSQL only: the case declares no other dialect, because top is a silent no-op everywhere else (see 'top is silently ignored by the non-mssql dialects'). Since 7.0.0 removed the automatic cap, an explicit top is the only thing that can put a TOP in a SELECT list at all.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];

cases.name: select scalar subquery whose value precedes the where value
cases.note: PARAM ORDERING. The subquery in the SELECT list binds 'shipped' and the outer WHERE binds true. Clauses are rendered SELECT-then-FROM-then-WHERE, so the subquery's value must be param 0 and the WHERE's value param 1 — Postgres numbers them $1/$2 in that order, and MSSQL declares `@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 bit`. A port that collects bound values per-clause and concatenates WHERE values before SELECT values produces syntactically valid SQL with the arguments silently transposed: the worst possible failure, because nothing throws.

cases.name: select scalar subquery carries no trailing semicolon
cases.note: The SELECT-list subquery is rendered by a fresh defaultToSql with `isInnerStatement` set, and that flag's only remaining job here is to suppress the trailing `;` — the statement has exactly one, at the very end, and a port that forgets the flag emits a stray semicolon inside the parentheses on every dialect. The cap is gone on all four dialects, the MSSQL golden is now the same shape as the other three, and the semicolon is the whole test.

cases.name: select column name embedding every dialect delimiter
cases.note: Identifier escaping is per-dialect and doubles ONLY that dialect's own closing delimiter: MSSQL doubles `]`, MySQL doubles the backtick, Postgres and SQLite double `"`. The other two delimiter characters pass through untouched in each dialect's output — so the same input column name yields four genuinely different strings. Getting this wrong is an injection hole, not a cosmetic bug: an unescaped closing delimiter lets a hostile column or alias name break out of the quoting.

cases.name: from derived table carrying a bound value
cases.note: The derived table's bound value must be spliced into the OUTER param list, ahead of the outer WHERE's value. If a port renders the subquery's SQL but drops its values, the placeholders still ship and every later parameter shifts by one — the query then binds NULL where it meant `true` and silently returns the wrong rows. Postgres is the loudest failure ($1/$2 renumbering); MySQL/SQLite fail silently.

cases.name: where group containing an in subquery with values on both sides
cases.note: The parameter-order case that matters most: a subquery nested inside a GROUP, which is itself a sub-builder. Values must come out 1, 100, true, 21 — the group's own values are spliced into the parent at the point the group is emitted, and the subquery's values are spliced into the group at the point IT is emitted. cases.name: limit only without an order by
cases.note: Highest-value divergence in this area, and the one that pins the top()/limit() split. MySQL/Postgres/SQLite emit `LIMIT 10` — legal and unordered. MSSQL renders EVERY limit as OFFSET/FETCH pagination, and T-SQL accepts OFFSET/FETCH only alongside an ORDER BY (Msg 102), so MSSQL REJECTS this outright and the case pins an error rather than SQL. `.limit()` is pagination and needs an order to page against; `.top(n)` is the designer's manual, SQL-Server-only row cap and the tool for an unordered one. A port that quietly emits `TOP (10)` here instead of throwing has conflated the two, and produces a query the caller never asked for. Since 7.0.0 that guard is the only thing standing between this op-list and a TOP: before, a port could have explained the missing TOP away as the automatic cap's explicit-limit suppression rule, which no longer exists.

mssql.throws: LimitOffset: ORDER BY is required when using LIMIT on MSSQL, which paginates with OFFSET/FETCH; use top() for an unordered row cap

cases.note: Byte-exact whitespace matters: the LIMIT..OFFSET dialects emit a DOUBLE space — `LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20` — because the emitter appends a separator space and then a snippet that already begins with one. Ugly, and frozen. MSSQL instead emits `OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY`, i.e. the two numbers appear in the OPPOSITE order to LIMIT..OFFSET; swapping them is an easy port bug that still produces valid SQL and wrong pages.

cases.name: offset only with an order by and no where clause
cases.note: Offset without a limit, and the four grammars that disagree about how to spell it. Postgres is the only dialect with a legal standalone OFFSET, so it emits a bare `  OFFSET 20` — note the LEADING double space, deliberate and pinned in detail by 'offset with a where and no limit: only postgres keeps the doubled space'. MySQL and SQLite cannot parse a bare OFFSET at all (ERROR 1064 / `near "OFFSET"`), so each emits its own "no upper bound" sentinel limit in front of it: `LIMIT 18446744073709551615` (2^64-1, the documented MySQL idiom — 2^64 is itself a syntax error) and `LIMIT -1`. Those sentinels are grammar, not a cap: they bound nothing, and they are followed by a single space because the extra separator is only added when an explicit limit was set. MSSQL emits `OFFSET 20 ROWS` with no FETCH, which is valid T-SQL. One intent, four spellings; a port that picks one and applies it everywhere breaks three dialects. Two dead contracts a port must not resurrect: 6.x synthesized a `LIMIT 1000` / `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY` cap here because there was no WHERE, and before 6.0.0 MSSQL emitted `TOP (1000) ... OFFSET 20 ROWS`, which SQL Server never accepted. 7.0.0 removed the cap outright.

Until 7.0.0 a WHERE was what marked a query 'bounded' and suppressed the automatic row cap, so the two cases had different tails everywhere: no WHERE meant `LIMIT 1000` / `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY`, a WHERE meant the sentinel-or-bare-OFFSET rendering seen here. 7.0.0 removed the cap, and with it the only reason defaultLimitOffset ever read `whereStates`. cases.note: 0 is the sentinel for 'no limit' — not a request for zero rows. to-sql only calls the limit/offset parser when `limit > 0 || offset > 0`, so limit(0) emits no LIMIT clause and the golden is byte-identical to the seed case on all four dialects. A Dart port modelling limit as `int?` must make null and 0 behave identically here, or it will emit `LIMIT 0` and return nothing. Before 7.0.0 the case had a second half: MSSQL's safety net tested `limit === 0`, so limit(0) brought `TOP (1000)` BACK and the MSSQL golden was not the seed case's. cases.name: mssql top with a where clause
cases.note: TOP and WHERE in one statement: the TOP goes in through the select hook and lands before the column list, the WHERE is appended after the FROM — `SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0`. Until 7.0.0 the case earned its keep by pinning a precedence: an explicit TOP beat both the automatic cap and the WHERE-suppresses-the-cap rule, so `TOP (5)` survived in a position where `TOP (1000)` would have been suppressed. With the cap gone there is no precedence left to resolve — an explicit top renders whenever it is set and positive, nothing else ever writes a TOP, and the WHERE is irrelevant to it.

cases.name: mssql top combined with limit is refused
cases.note: defaultLimitOffset throws when MSSQL has both a `top` in customState and a limit/offset: they are two different pagination mechanisms and T-SQL rejects them in one SELECT (Msg 10741). The subtle pin is WHICH error. This op-list trips two guards — it has a limit and no ORDER BY, so MSSQL's 'ORDER BY is required when using LIMIT' guard is eligible too — but the TOP/LIMIT conflict is raised while the clause text is still being built and the ORDER BY guards only after it, so the TOP/LIMIT message is the one a port must produce. Note also that the conflict check reads customState presence, not the rendered SQL, so a port must track 'top was set by the caller' as its own state; see 'top zero is absent for rendering but present for the limit conflict check', where a top that renders nothing still throws. (The pre-7.0.0 note here justified the presence test by claiming the implicit TOP (1000) coexisted with an OFFSET elsewhere in the corpus and therefore never threw. That was never true — MSSQL spelled the cap `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY` precisely because TOP and OFFSET cannot share a SELECT. The presence test is right for the reason above, not that one.)

mssql.throws: LimitOffset: MSSQL should not use both TOP and LIMIT/OFFSET in the same query

cases.name: top is silently ignored by the non-mssql dialects
cases.note: top() lives on the shared builder, not on the MSSQL builder, so it is callable everywhere — and everywhere else it is a no-op: no TOP, no LIMIT, no clause at all. The golden is byte-identical to the seed case. A Dart port that throws 'unsupported' on top() for MySQL, or that translates it into LIMIT, diverges. It also must not throw the TOP+LIMIT error outside MSSQL — that guard lives inside the MSSQL branch of defaultLimitOffset. (The old note explained the empty tail by saying these three dialects had no unbounded-query safety net, unlike MSSQL. Since 7.0.0 no dialect has one: an unbounded SELECT is unbounded everywhere unless the caller caps it.)

cases.name: subquery with group by having order by and limit
cases.note: An EXPLICIT limit inside a subquery is rendered, and rendered per dialect: `LIMIT 5` on MySQL/Postgres/SQLite, `OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 5 ROWS ONLY` on MSSQL — note MSSQL pays for a limit with an OFFSET the caller never gave, because T-SQL has no FETCH without one. The subquery's orderByRaw is what makes that legal: MSSQL's OFFSET/FETCH requires an ORDER BY (Msg 102) and the guard reads the inner state, so dropping the inner order by would turn this case into a throw. The IN subquery is an inner statement and carries no trailing `;`, so the statement has exactly one, at the very end. (Before 7.0.0 the note also said inner statements were exempt from the automatic cap, and that the outer query's IN counted as a WHERE and so 'bounded' it. Both rules are gone: no statement gets a cap, inner or outer, and nothing reads whereStates for pagination.)

cases.name: update set values bind before where values
cases.note: THE ordering pin for UPDATE. SET values are emitted before WHERE values, so they bind first: Postgres numbers the SET values $1, $2 and the WHERE values $3, $4, and the positional dialects bind [John, 31, 42, 'deleted'] in exactly that order. setRaw contributes NO bound value and NO placeholder — it must not consume an index. A port that renders WHERE before SET, or that counts setRaw as a parameter, produces an UPDATE that writes the wrong values to the wrong rows and still executes without error.

cases.note: set()/setRaw() do not make the builder an UPDATE — only updateTable/updateTableWithOwner do, and both push the table at the same time. So the parser's 'UPDATE requires a table' guard (and the identical DELETE one) is unreachable through the public API: omit updateTable and the builder is still a SELECT, failing in the SELECT parser. The message pinned here is the one a port must reproduce — not a friendlier 'UPDATE requires a table'.

cases.name: delete with alias and where
cases.note: The headline DELETE divergence, mirroring UPDATE: T-SQL has no `DELETE FROM tbl AS alias`, so MSSQL emits `DELETE [u] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE ...` while the other three emit `DELETE FROM tbl AS u WHERE ...`. The Delete branch of to-sql returns as soon as it has written the WHERE and the semicolon, so nothing downstream of it — the select hook, limit/offset — can reach a write statement at all. That structural early return, not a rule about writes, is why MSSQL's TOP has never appeared on a DELETE. (Before 7.0.0 the same early return was also what kept the automatic `TOP (1000)` off an unfiltered DELETE.)

cases.name: cte with a bound value binds before the outer query
cases.note: Pins parameter ORDER, not just SQL shape. cases.name: recursive cte with a union all body
cases.note: The canonical recursive shape: anchor UNION ALL recursive member, inside the CTE body. Two things are pinned. (1) `WITH RECURSIVE` is emitted for EVERY dialect, including MSSQL — where `RECURSIVE` is not a T-SQL keyword and the statement would not actually run. That is the frozen contract; a port must not 'fix' it. (2) Value order inside the body: the anchor's value binds before the recursive member's, and both bind before any outer value.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH RECURSIVE [descendants] AS (SELECT [e].[id], [e].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e] WHERE [e].[id] = @p0 UNION ALL SELECT [e2].[id], [e2].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e2] WHERE [e2].[depth] < @p1) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE [d].[id] <> @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 10, @p2 = 1;

postgres.raw: WITH RECURSIVE "descendants" AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = 1 UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e2" WHERE "e2"."depth" < 10) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE "d"."id" <> 1;

cases.name: cte referenced with fromTable is schema qualified
cases.note: A trap, pinned deliberately. `fromTable` always applies the dialect's default owner, so referencing a CTE that way emits `[dbo].[active_users]` on MSSQL and "public"."active_users" on Postgres — which does not resolve to the CTE. MySQL and SQLite have no default owner and come out fine. The dialect-neutral way to reference a CTE is fromRaw. A port must reproduce the qualification, not silently special-case CTE names.

cases.name: mssql top is emitted inside a union branch but dropped inside a cte body
cases.note: An asymmetry in the MSSQL TOP hook, frozen as-is. The union parser threads the dialect's `beforeSelectColumns` hook into each branch, so an explicit `.top(3)` on a union branch renders `SELECT TOP (3)`. The CTE parser does NOT thread the hook, so the identical `.top(5)` on the CTE body renders nothing at all — the golden's CTE is a plain `SELECT [u].[id]`. Being an inner statement is not the reason: the hook never looks at `isInnerStatement`, which is exactly why the union branch keeps its TOP despite being one. Reaching the hook is the whole difference. A Dart port that threads the hook uniformly through both emits an extra TOP in the CTE and diverges. Since 7.0.0 removed the automatic cap, every TOP in this statement is one the caller wrote; the golden has exactly one, on the union branch.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [capped] AS (SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]) SELECT [c].[id] FROM capped AS c WHERE [c].[id] > @p0 UNION ALL SELECT TOP (3) [a].[id] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a];', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 0;

mssql.raw: WITH [capped] AS (SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]) SELECT [c].[id] FROM capped AS c WHERE [c].[id] > 0 UNION ALL SELECT TOP (3) [a].[id] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a];

cases.name: cte outer query and cte body both render without a top
cases.note: Frozen mainly for what is NOT here. Until 7.0.0 MSSQL's unbounded-query guard looked only at the OUTER statement — no WHERE, no LIMIT, not an inner statement — so `TOP (1000)` landed on this outer SELECT even though the real row source is the CTE, whose own WHERE could never reach the guard. 7.0.0 removed the cap: the golden now has no TOP anywhere, and a port that reintroduces one fails here. What the case still pins on its own: the CTE name is quoted in the WITH clause (`WITH [active_users] AS`) while the fromRaw reference to it is emitted verbatim and unquoted (`FROM active_users AS au`) — which is why fromRaw is the dialect-neutral way to reach a CTE, and 'cte referenced with fromTable is schema qualified' is the same query through fromTable, where the reference gets schema-qualified into something that does not resolve. The CTE body is an inner statement, so the statement's only `;` is the outer one.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = @p0) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'active';

mssql.raw: WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au;

cases.name: union of two selects each with a bound value
cases.note: Set-operation branches bind AFTER the outer query's own WHERE values (the union clause is walked after WHERE/GROUP BY/HAVING), so the params come out 'active' then 'pending' — outer first — and MSSQL numbers them `@p0`/`@p1` in that same order. Each branch is rendered as an inner statement, so no branch carries a trailing semicolon and the statement has exactly one, at the end. (Before 7.0.0 the inner-statement flag also kept the MSSQL TOP and the MySQL/Postgres/SQLite LIMIT safety nets out of each branch. There are no safety nets now, so the semicolon and the bind order are the whole test.)

cases.name: multi builder batch of insert update and select
cases.note: The whole point of a batch: placeholder numbering RESTARTS at each statement, because each is prepared independently (Postgres emits $1,$2 then $1,$2 then $1 — not $1..$5; MSSQL emits a fresh `SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql` with @p0 per statement). cases.note: The biggest Dart landmine in the corpus. JS `Date.toISOString()` ALWAYS yields UTC, exactly 3 fractional digits, trailing Z. Dart's `DateTime.toIso8601String()` does neither: on a LOCAL DateTime it omits the Z and does NOT convert (a device at UTC+2 silently shifts the value two hours), and it emits 6 fractional digits whenever microseconds are non-zero. cases.note: Past 2^53 an integer is not exactly representable as a double, so MSSQL declares it `float`, NOT `bigint`. cases.note: Load-bearing for the entire placeholder scheme. The internal placeholder token is NUL-delimited, so a raw fragment carrying NUL could FORGE a token and steal a bound value's slot. cases.name: nul byte in a bound value diverges by dialect
cases.note: A deliberate divergence that must be reproduced exactly. A NUL inside a VALUE is not rejected: values bypass the fragment path that screens for NUL, so MySQL/Postgres/SQLite bind the string with its NUL intact and leave it to the driver. MSSQL differs because it inlines values into the sp_executesql text (to-sql.ts appends `@pN = <value>` via the fragment path), so there the NUL reaches the fragment check and the whole statement is refused. Do not 'harmonize' this by rejecting NUL values everywhere, and do not let MSSQL emit one.

cases.name: every clause contributing a value pins the full parameter order
cases.note: The single strongest ordering assertion in the corpus. to-sql walks CTE -> SELECT -> FROM -> JOIN -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> UNION -> ORDER BY, and each clause splices its sub-values in that order. Here seven values are bound from seven different clauses: the CTE body (10.5), a scalar subquery in the SELECT list ('click'), a derived table in FROM (true), a joinOn VALUE (100), the outer WHERE (0), the HAVING (999) and the UNION branch (2020). Expected order is exactly that. No authored case pins more than two clauses at once, so a port that, say, collects join values after where values, or hoists HAVING before GROUP BY, still emits byte-identical SQL on MySQL/SQLite and silently binds the wrong rows. Postgres is the only dialect where the mis-order is even visible ($n renumbering), and MSSQL's inlined sp_executesql values make it visible too.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [recent] AS (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > @p0) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[logs] AS [l] WHERE [l].[kind] = @p1) AS [click_count], [au].[id] FROM (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p2) AS [au] INNER JOIN [dbo].[payments] AS [p] ON [au].[id] = [p].[user_id] AND [p].[amount] > @p3 WHERE [au].[id] > @p4 GROUP BY [au].[id] HAVING [au].[id] < @p5 UNION ALL SELECT [x].[id] FROM [dbo].[archive] AS [x] WHERE [x].[year] = @p6;', N'@p0 float, @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 bit, @p3 tinyint, @p4 tinyint, @p5 smallint, @p6 smallint', @p0 = 10.5, @p1 = N'click', @p2 = 1, @p3 = 100, @p4 = 0, @p5 = 999, @p6 = 2020;

cases.name: a union branch that carries its own cte emits WITH inside the UNION
cases.note: defaultUnion renders each branch with the full defaultToSql, and that walk emits the branch's cteStates FIRST — so a CTE declared inside a union branch produces `... UNION ALL WITH "t" AS (...) SELECT ...`, which no engine accepts. Frozen contract: reproduce it, do not hoist the CTE to the head of the statement. Also pins the value order across the boundary: the OUTER where value binds before the branch's CTE value, which binds before the branch's own where value.

cases.name: an explicit top inside a derived table is silently dropped
cases.note: MSSQL's TOP is injected by the `beforeSelectColumns` hook in ToSqlOptions, and defaultFrom/defaultJoin/defaultSelect/defaultWhere all call `defaultToSql(sub, config, mode)` WITHOUT passing options. So `.top(5)` inside a fromWithBuilder produces NO TOP at all — the row cap the caller explicitly asked for vanishes. Compare with the next case, where the union parser DOES thread options through and the same op does render. Since 7.0.0 the MSSQL golden contains no TOP whatsoever and is byte-identical to 'from derived table', which is this op-list minus the top: the pin is now the strong one, that `.top(5)` here changes nothing at all. (Before 7.0.0 the outer SELECT still collected the automatic `TOP (1000)`, so the pin was the weaker 'exactly one TOP, and it is not the one the caller wrote'.) A Dart port that threads its options object uniformly through every subquery emits `TOP (5)` in the derived table and fails.

The MSSQL golden has exactly one TOP and it is the branch's explicit one; since 7.0.0 removed the automatic cap that is the only kind of TOP there is, and the outer query's WHERE has nothing to do with it. cases.note: TOP is emitted by the select hook, so it lands before the column list and BEFORE the JOIN clauses — `SELECT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN ...`. The three other dialects ignore top entirely and put no LIMIT in its place — their goldens are the plain joined SELECT. (Before 7.0.0 this was the contract in the opposite direction: the explicit top suppressed MSSQL's automatic cap, and the note called it out that an unbounded MySQL/Postgres/SQLite SELECT was genuinely unbounded while the MSSQL one was capped. That asymmetry is gone; all four are unbounded unless the caller says otherwise.)

cases.note: Two different tests read customState['top']. The select hook requires `top > 0` to render, so top(0) renders nothing. The MSSQL limit/offset guard only tests presence (`!== null && !== undefined`), so top(0) DOES trip 'MSSQL should not use both TOP and LIMIT/OFFSET in the same query'. MSSQL therefore THROWS here while MySQL/Postgres/SQLite ignore the top and happily emit LIMIT 10. A Dart port modelling top as `int?` and treating 0 as unset never throws; one that treats it as set renders `TOP (0)`. Both fail.

cases.name: a negative limit is ignored
cases.note: to-sql only calls the limit/offset parser when `limit > 0 || offset > 0`, so limit(-1) emits no LIMIT clause anywhere and the golden is byte-identical to the seed case on all four dialects. The trap is that -1 is not inert everywhere it might be passed through: it is SQLite's own spelling of 'no upper bound', and this library uses it as exactly that (see the sentinel in 'offset only with an order by and no where clause'), so a port that forwards the value instead of dropping it emits a clause that is accidentally harmless on SQLite and wrong elsewhere. The contract is that nothing is emitted. (Before 7.0.0 the case had a second half: MSSQL's safety net required `limit === 0` and -1 is not 0, so a negative limit was the only route to a completely uncapped MSSQL SELECT and a port that clamped it to 0 re-enabled `TOP (1000)`. The cap is gone, clamping -1 to 0 is now indistinguishable, and this case no longer catches it.)

cases.name: offset without a limit on an unfiltered query emits no cap
cases.note: The case that moved most in 7.0.0. In 6.x this was the ONLY route to the MySQL/Postgres/SQLite automatic LIMIT: defaultLimitOffset ran (offset > 0), saw limit === 0, no inner statement and no WHERE, and emitted `LIMIT 1000 OFFSET 5`; MSSQL reached the same cap down its own branch and spelled it `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY`, with no TOP, since TOP and OFFSET cannot share a SELECT (Msg 10741). A caller paging an unfiltered table got 1000 rows and no sign the rest existed. 7.0.0 removed the cap: Postgres now emits a bare `  OFFSET 5` and returns every row past the fifth, MySQL and SQLite emit only their sentinel limits — which bound nothing — and MSSQL emits `OFFSET 5 ROWS` with no FETCH. The golden is byte-identical to 'offset with a where and no limit: only postgres keeps the doubled space' apart from the WHERE, because the WHERE no longer touches the tail; a port whose two goldens differ has kept a cap somewhere.

cases.name: offset with a where and no limit: only postgres keeps the doubled space
cases.note: SPACING, byte-for-byte. to-sql prefixes the limit/offset helper with " ", and the helper's offset branch starts with ANOTHER leading space (" OFFSET "). Postgres is the one dialect that accepts a standalone OFFSET and therefore emits nothing between the two spaces, so the result is `... ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 5;` with TWO spaces. A port that normalizes whitespace, or builds the clause list by joining on " ", collapses it and fails. MySQL and SQLite fill that gap with their sentinel limit (`LIMIT 18446744073709551615` / `LIMIT -1`) and so emit a single space; MSSQL's branch has no leading space and also emits one. The doubled space long predates 7.0.0 and is not a symptom of the removed cap — it is simply what an empty limit slot looks like. What 7.0.0 changed is how many queries can reach it: compare 'offset without a limit on an unfiltered query emits no cap', which is this case without the WHERE and now has the same two spaces, where 6.x filled the slot with `LIMIT 1000`.

cases.name: identifiers containing every dialect's closing delimiter and a quote
cases.note: quoteIdentifier doubles ONLY the dialect's own closing delimiter: `]`->`]]` on MSSQL, `"`->`""` on Postgres/SQLite, backtick->double-backtick on MySQL. The same name therefore escapes differently in each dialect and passes the other delimiters through untouched — one shared 'escape all the quote chars' helper in a port is wrong everywhere. The apostrophe adds a second layer: MSSQL wraps the finished statement in sp_executesql and doubles EVERY single quote in the SQL text, including the ones inside identifiers, so `al'ias` becomes `[al''ias]` there and stays `"al'ias"` elsewhere. The dotted alias pins that a `.` inside a name is quoted as one identifier, never split.

cases.name: an integral double and an int render identically in typescript
cases.note: THE Dart divergence. JavaScript has one number type, so `{t:'double', v:2}` and `{t:'int', v:2}` are the same value: both inline as `2` in parseRaw and both declare `@pN tinyint` in MSSQL (Number.isSafeInteger(2.0) is true). Dart's `2.0` is a genuine double: `toString()` gives `2.0` and any type inference sees a double, so a Dart driver that faithfully constructs a double from the `double` tag will emit `2.0` / `float` and fail — while one that collapses both tags to `int` passes this case but fails the fractional ones. The correct Dart driver must construct a double AND render it through the same integral-collapsing path TypeScript uses. -3 is included so the tinyint/smallint boundary is crossed by both tags at once.

cases.note: Number rendering, the other half. 1e21 is an integer by `Number.isInteger` but NOT by `Number.isSafeInteger`, so MSSQL declares it `float` (deliberately — src/parser/to-sql.ts notes that `bigint` has no scientific-notation literal and SQL Server rejected the batch) and inlines it as `1e+21` via toString(). 1.5e-7 renders `1.5e-7`; 0.1 renders `0.1`. cases.name: datetime precision below a millisecond is truncated
cases.note: JavaScript's Date holds MILLISECONDS; Dart's DateTime holds MICROSECONDS. Given `...12:00:00.123456Z`, `new Date(...).toISOString()` yields `.123Z` — the extra digits are gone from both the bound param and MSSQL's inlined literal. Dart's `DateTime.parse` keeps them and `toIso8601String()` emits `.123456Z`, so the Dart driver MUST truncate to milliseconds at the boundary. Also pins a pre-epoch instant (negative epoch ms, a classic overflow/format trap) and a whereBetween of two datetimes, whose two values must bind low-then-high between the surrounding predicates.

Each level is rendered by a fresh defaultToSql and its values are spliced into the parent at the position of its placeholder, so the order is 50 (inner-1 where), 5 (inner-2 where), then the outer 'x'. Both inner statements are marked isInnerStatement, so neither gets a trailing ';' — the whole statement has exactly one, at the very end. cases.name: an empty where group emits empty parentheses
cases.note: whereGroup(() => {}) still pushes its three states, so whereStates is non-empty and the query renders `WHERE ()` — no predicate, no bound value, on all four dialects. The parentheses are emitted even though the child produced no SQL: the GroupBuilder branch splices an empty string rather than pruning the group, and a port that prunes empty groups emits a bare `SELECT * FROM users` and fails. Until 7.0.0 there was a second and sharper consequence: because whereStates.length > 0, MSSQL's safety-net TOP did not fire, so an unfiltered SELECT lost its row cap simply by adding a group that filtered nothing. That was the plainest demonstration that the cap keyed off the wrong thing, and it is part of why the cap is gone.

cases.name: whereNotExists ignores the table and column it is given
cases.note: The signature is `whereNotExistsWithBuilder(table, column, builder)` and the parser uses NEITHER: the NotExists branch of defaultWhere emits `NOT EXISTS (` + subquery + `)` and never touches tableNameOrAlias/columnName. The deliberately absurd arguments here must not appear anywhere in the output on any dialect. A Dart port that helpfully renders `"IGNORED"."ALSO_IGNORED" NOT EXISTS (...)`, or that validates the arguments, diverges. The correlated predicate inside the subquery is the only thing tying it to the outer row.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND [o].[total] > @p0);', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 9;

cases.name: a cte in front of an update binds before the set and where values
cases.note: to-sql emits the WITH clause BEFORE it branches on queryType, so a CTE attached to an UPDATE renders `WITH "stale" AS (SELECT ...) UPDATE ...` — and its values occupy the FIRST parameter slots, ahead of the SET value and then the WHERE value. Nothing in the authored corpus combines a CTE with a write statement; a port that builds writes through a separate code path drops the WITH entirely (silently updating everything) or binds the CTE value last. On MSSQL this also stacks with the aliased-UPDATE rewrite: `UPDATE [u] SET ... FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE ...`, with the FROM appended after the SET list.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [stale] AS (SELECT [s].[id] FROM [dbo].[sessions] AS [s] WHERE [s].[seen_at] < @p0) UPDATE [u] SET [status] = @p1 FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] > @p2;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 tinyint', @p0 = '2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z', @p1 = N'expired', @p2 = 10;

mssql.raw: WITH [stale] AS (SELECT [s].[id] FROM [dbo].[sessions] AS [s] WHERE [s].[seen_at] < 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z) UPDATE [u] SET [status] = expired FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] > 10;

prepared.sql: WITH `stale` AS (SELECT `s`.`id` FROM `sessions` AS `s` WHERE `s`.`seen_at` < ?) UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `status` = ? WHERE `u`.`id` > ?;

cases.name: update with an owner on mysql does not throw and silently discards join order by and limit
cases.note: Two divergences in one statement. (1) The MySQL owner guard lives ONLY in default-from.ts — default-update.ts has no such check, so `updateTableWithOwner('sales', ...)` emits `sales`.`orders` on MySQL where the identical FROM would throw 'MySQL does not support table owners'. A port that centralizes the owner check throws here and fails. (2) The Update branch of to-sql returns immediately after UPDATE/SET/WHERE, so the joinTable, orderByColumn and limit ops on this builder produce NOTHING — no error, no SQL, and the join's onValue (7) is NOT bound. Params are exactly [9.5, 1]. A port whose write path shares the SELECT clause walk emits a JOIN and an extra parameter.

mssql.raw: UPDATE [o] SET [total] = 9.5 FROM [sales].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[id] = 1;

cases.name: multi builder restarts placeholder numbering per statement and concatenates without a separator
cases.note: parseMulti loops `sql += parse(state, config)` — each statement is prepared INDEPENDENTLY, so Postgres numbers both statements from $1 (the second statement's values are NOT $3/$4) and MSSQL emits two complete `SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql ...` batches. There is NO separator between statements: each ends with ';' and the next begins immediately, so the golden contains `...;INSERT` with no space, while the transaction prologue DOES add one (`BEGIN; `). Note also that parseMulti closes with `COMMIT;` while parseMultiRaw closes with `COMMIT; ` — a trailing space in raw only. A port that joins statements with '\n' or ' ', or that threads one placeholder counter across the batch, fails.

prepared.sql: BEGIN TRANSACTION; SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'INSERT INTO [dbo].[users] ([name], [age]) VALUES (@p0, @p1);', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint', @p0 = N'Ada', @p1 = 36;SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [dbo].[users] SET [active] = @p0 WHERE [users].[name] = @p1;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'Ada';COMMIT TRANSACTION;
